text: This study and a companion study of anti–PD-L1 antibody, now reported in the Journal , 26 describe clinical activity with these agents that validates the importance of the PD-1–PD-L1 pathway for the treatment of some cancers. The signals of clinical activity in patients with non–small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer treated with anti–PD-1 antibody and the possibility of basing patient selection for such treatment on PD-L1 expression in tumors need to be prospectively assessed. Phase 2 trials involving immunologic and molecular-marker correlates (ClinicalTrials.gov numbers, NCT01354431 and NCT01358721 ) are under way, and phase 3 studies of anti–PD-1 antibody for the treatment of non–small-cell lung cancer, melanoma, and renal-cell cancer are being planned. An assessment of such treatment for other tumor types is also of interest.
summary: The PD-1–PD-L1 pathway is identified as important for treating some cancers, and clinical activity has been observed in patients with melanoma treated with an anti–PD-1 antibody. The proposed mechanism is blockade of PD-1, and PD-L1 expression in tumors is suggested as a possible basis for patient selection. These signals of clinical activity and the biomarker-based selection approach need prospective assessment. Phase 2 trials with immunologic and molecular-marker correlates are under way, and phase 3 studies of anti–PD-1 antibody for melanoma are being planned.

text: Two patients (one with melanoma who received 3.0 mg of anti–PD-1 antibody per kilogram and one with renal-cell cancer who received 10.0 mg per kilogram) had a complete response.
summary: A patient with melanoma who received 3.0 mg of anti–PD-1 antibody per kilogram had a complete response.

text: The median time to the peak concentration of anti PD-1 antibody was 1 to 4 hours after the start of infusion. The pharmacokinetics of the antibody were linear, with a dose-proportional increase in the peak concentration and area under the curve calculated from day 1 to day 14 in the dose range of 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram (35 patients). The pharmacodynamics of anti–PD-1 antibody were assessed according to PD-1–receptor occupancy on circulating CD3+ T cells. In PBMCs from 65 patients with melanoma who were treated with one cycle of anti–PD-1 antibody at a dose of 0.1 to 10.0 mg per kilogram every 2 weeks, the median PD-1–receptor occupancy by anti–PD-1 antibody was 64 to 70% according to dose level ( Fig. 2A ).
summary: Median time to peak concentration of anti–PD-1 antibody was 1 to 4 hours after infusion start, and pharmacokinetics were linear with dose-proportional increases in peak concentration and area under the curve from day 1 to day 14 in the 0.1–10.0 mg/kg range (35 patients). Pharmacodynamics were assessed by PD-1–receptor occupancy on circulating CD3+ T cells in PBMCs from 65 patients with melanoma given one cycle of anti–PD-1 antibody (0.1–10.0 mg/kg every 2 weeks). Median PD-1–receptor occupancy was 64–70% by dose level.
